content:
# Genesis Chapter 27 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 15r–15v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_027_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_027_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), 4th century CE — LXX | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0027 (page 23), https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0028 (page 24)

---

## The Stolen Blessing

**1** When Isaac was old and his eyes had grown so dim he could no longer see, he called for his older son Esau. "My son," he said.

"Here I am," said Esau.

**2** Isaac said, "Look at me — I am old. I don't know the day I will die. **3** So take your gear — your quiver and your bow — and go out into the open country and hunt some game for me. **4** Make me the kind of savory food I love. Bring it to me so I can eat, and then I will give you my blessing before I die."

---

**5** Now Rebekah had been listening while Isaac spoke to his son Esau. As soon as Esau left for the countryside to hunt, **6** Rebekah went to her son Jacob. "I just heard your father say to your brother Esau: **7** 'Bring me some game and make me a savory dish. I will eat it, and then I will bless you in the presence of the LORD before I die.' **8** So now, my son, listen to me — do exactly what I tell you. **9** Go out to the flock and bring me two young goats. I will make them into the savory dish your father loves. **10** You will carry it to your father, he will eat, and then he will give you his blessing before he dies."

**11** Jacob said to his mother Rebekah, "But my brother Esau is a hairy man, and I have smooth skin. **12** What if my father touches me? He will know I am trying to deceive him, and I will bring a curse on myself instead of a blessing."

**13** His mother said, "Let that curse fall on me, my son. Just do what I say. Go get the goats."

**14** So he went and got them and brought them to his mother, and she made the savory dish his father loved. **15** Then Rebekah took the best clothes of her older son Esau — the clothes she kept stored in the house — and dressed her younger son Jacob in them. **16** She covered his hands and the smooth part of his neck with the skins of the young goats. **17** Then she handed the savory food and the bread she had made to her son Jacob.

---

**18** He went in to his father and said, "Father."

"Yes," said his father. "Who are you, my son?"

**19** Jacob said to his father, "I am Esau, your firstborn. I have done what you asked. Please sit up and eat my game so that you may give me your blessing."

**20** Isaac said to his son, "How did you find it so quickly?"

"The LORD your God put it in my path," Jacob said.

**21** Then Isaac said to Jacob, "Come close so I can feel you, my son — to know whether you really are my son Esau or not." **22** Jacob went close to his father, and Isaac felt him and said, "The voice is Jacob's voice, but the hands are Esau's hands." **23** He did not recognize him, because his hands were hairy like his brother Esau's hands. So he went ahead and blessed him. **24** "Are you truly my son Esau?" he asked.

"I am," said Jacob.

**25** "Bring me the food," said his father. "I will eat my son's game and then give you my blessing." Jacob brought it and he ate. He also brought him wine, and he drank.

**26** Then his father Isaac said to him, "Come close now and kiss me, my son." **27** He went close and kissed him. When Isaac smelled the scent of his clothing, he blessed him:

"Ah, the scent of my son —
    like the scent of a field the LORD has blessed.
**28** May God give you the dew of the sky
    and the richness of the earth —
    abundance of grain and new wine.
**29** May peoples serve you,
    and nations bow down to you.
Be lord over your brothers,
    and may your mother's sons bow before you.
Cursed be everyone who curses you,
    and blessed be everyone who blesses you."

---

**30** Isaac had barely finished blessing Jacob, and Jacob had barely stepped out from before his father, when his brother Esau came in from the hunt. **31** He also prepared a savory dish and brought it to his father. "Father, sit up and eat your son's game," he said, "so you may give me your blessing."

**32** His father Isaac said to him, "Who are you?"

"I am your son," he said, "your firstborn — Esau."

**33** Isaac was seized with violent trembling. "Then who was it," he said, "who hunted game and brought it to me? I ate it all before you came, and I blessed him — and he will stay blessed."

**34** When Esau heard his father's words, he let out a wail that was loud and bitter beyond anything. "Bless me too, father! Bless me too!" he said.

**35** But Isaac said, "Your brother came by deception and took your blessing."

**36** Esau said, "Is it any wonder his name is Jacob? He has tripped me up twice now — he took my birthright, and now he has taken my blessing." Then he said, "Haven't you saved any blessing for me?"

**37** Isaac answered Esau: "I have made him lord over you, and I have given him all his brothers as servants. I have given him grain and wine to sustain him. What is there left to give you, my son?"

**38** Esau said to his father, "Is that the only blessing you have, father? Bless me too, father!" And Esau wept aloud.

**39** Then his father Isaac answered him:

"Your home will be far from the richness of the earth,
    far from the dew of the sky above.
**40** You will live by your sword,
    and you will serve your brother.
But when you finally break free,
    you will tear his yoke from your neck."

---

**41** From that day on, Esau bore a grudge against Jacob because of the blessing his father had given him. Esau told himself, "The days of mourning for my father are coming soon. Then I will kill my brother Jacob."

**42** When Rebekah was told what her older son Esau was saying, she sent for her younger son Jacob and told him, "Your brother Esau is consoling himself with plans to kill you. **43** So now, my son, listen to me: get yourself up and flee to my brother Laban in Haran. **44** Stay with him for a while, until your brother's fury subsides — **45** until your brother's anger turns away from you and he forgets what you did to him. Then I will send word and bring you back. Why should I lose both of you in one day?"

**46** Rebekah then said to Isaac, "I am sick to death of these Hittite women. If Jacob also takes a wife from the Hittite women of this land — women like these — what will my life even be worth?"

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### The structure of catastrophe

Genesis 27 is the most tightly wound piece of dramatic irony in the patriarchal narratives. Everything turns on who knows what, and when. The reader knows from Genesis 25:23 — "the older will serve the younger" — that the LORD has already declared the outcome. And yet watching the mechanism by which that outcome arrives is almost unbearable, because everyone in the story is implicated: Isaac blunders forward in blind love for Esau; Rebekah schemes in cold-blooded urgency; Jacob lies to his father's face not once but three times; Esau comes too late and loses everything; and the blessing, once spoken, cannot be recalled. No one walks out of this chapter clean.

---

### Verse 1 — "his eyes were too dim to see" (*mikhehot 'einav*)

**וַיְהִי כִּי-זָקֵן יִצְחָק וַתִּכְהֶיןָ עֵינָיו מֵרְאֹת** — "And it was when Isaac was old, and his eyes were dim from seeing." The Hebrew verb **כהה** (*kahah*) means to grow faint, weak, dimmed — used elsewhere of fading lamps and extinguished fire. Isaac's blindness is literal: the Leningrad Codex reads the verb with perfect clarity. But it is also the engine of everything that follows. Without his blindness, the deception is impossible. The man who cannot see is about to confer the most consequential blessing in the story of Israel, and he will give it to the wrong son — or, depending on your reading, to the right son by the wrong means. The irony is dense and terrible from the very first clause.

---

### Verse 4 — "that my soul may bless you" (*vatevarekh'kha nafshi*)

**וְעָשֵׂה-לִּי מַטְעַמִּים כַּאֲשֶׁר אָהַבְתִּי וְהָבִיאָה לִּי וְאֹכֵלָה בַּעֲבוּר תְּבָרֶכְךָ נַפְשִׁי** — "And make for me delicacies as I love, and bring them to me and let me eat, so that my soul may bless you." The phrase **תְּבָרֶכְךָ נַפְשִׁי** (*tevarekh'kha nafshi*) — literally "my soul/life-force may bless you" — is not poetic embellishment. In the world of this text, the blessing is a quasi-physical act, an emanation of the inner life of the one blessing. It requires the full engagement of *nefesh* — the breathing, embodied, desiring, living person. Isaac is not signing a document. He is preparing to give away something of himself — something that, once given, cannot be retrieved. The food and the meal are part of the ritual: the body must be nourished and present for the blessing to go out with full force. This is why Rebekah's scheme has to work at the level of the senses — touch, smell, taste — not merely at the level of words.

---

### Verse 12 — "I would seem to him as a mocker" (*kimta'te'a*)

**אוּלַי יְמֻשֵּׁנִי אָבִי וְהָיִיתִי בְעֵינָיו כִּמְתַעְתֵּעַ** — "Perhaps my father will feel me and I will be in his eyes like one who deceives/mocks." The word **כִּמְתַעְתֵּעַ** (*kimta'te'a*) is from the root **תָּעַ** — to wander, go astray, be deceived. The Piel/Pilpel form here suggests a deliberate deceiver, a trickster, a mocker. Jacob's fear is that he will be seen as a fraud — not merely caught in a lie, but revealed as a person who has no dignity, who plays at sacred things. The irony is that in trying to avoid looking like a mocker, he proceeds to do exactly what a mocker does. His concern is not about the morality of the act; it is about getting caught. That detail alone tells you something important about Jacob at this stage of his life.

---

### Verse 13 — "Let your curse be on me" (*'alai qillatekha*)

**וַתֹּאמֶר לוֹ אִמּוֹ עָלַי קִלְלָתְךָ בְּנִי** — "And his mother said to him: Upon me your curse, my son." The brevity of Rebekah's declaration is stunning. Three Hebrew words: *'alai qillatekha beni* — "on me, your curse, my son." She does not argue, explain, theologize, or equivocate. She absorbs the risk into herself. She will carry the weight of the potential curse so her son can walk forward unburdened into the blessing. This is the most maternal line in the chapter — and also the most ruthlessly strategic. Rebekah is not merely a loving mother. She is a woman who has received a divine oracle (Genesis 25:23) and has decided she will enforce it by any means available to her. The character is simultaneously admirable and frightening.

---

### Verse 22 — the chapter's iconic line

**וַיֹּאמֶר יִצְחָק אֶל-יַעֲקֹב גְּשָׁה-נָּא וַאֲמֻשְּׁךָ בְּנִי הַקֹּל קוֹל יַעֲקֹב וְהַיָּדַיִם יְדֵי עֵשָׂו** — "And Isaac said to Jacob: Come near so I may feel you, my son. The voice is the voice of Jacob, but the hands are the hands of Esau." This is the line the entire narrative has been building toward. Isaac is so close to truth — he *hears* it in his son's voice — and yet he allows what his hands tell him to override what his ears tell him. He chooses the evidence of touch over the evidence of speech. The moment is a small tragedy of epistemology: the man who cannot see reaches out to feel the world, and the world has been arranged to deceive him.

The Vaticanus LXX reads **αἱ δὲ χεῖρες χεῖρες Ησαυ** — "the hands are the hands of Esau" — in close agreement with the Hebrew. The line's structure — "the voice / the voice of Jacob / but the hands / the hands of Esau" — is chiastic and rhythmic in both languages. It was clearly understood as a memorable formulation even in antiquity.

---

### Verse 27 — "like the smell of a field"

**וַיִּשְׁמַע אֶת-רֵיחַ בְּגָדָיו וַיְבָרֲכֵהוּ וַיֹּאמַר רְאֵה רֵיחַ בְּנִי כְּרֵיחַ שָׂדֶה** — "And he smelled the scent of his garments and blessed him, and said: See, the scent of my son is like the scent of a field." The word **רֵיחַ** (*reach*) — smell, scent, fragrance — appears three times in a single clause. Isaac is overwhelmed by sensation. The clothes Jacob wears are Esau's best clothes, which smell of the outdoors, of hunt and earth and grass. Isaac breathes in his son (or who he believes to be his son) and the blessing rises in him unbidden. The scent triggers the blessing. This is the genius of Rebekah's scheme: she knows her husband well enough to know that for a man who lives through his senses, a man now deprived of sight, scent will confirm what touch has already suggested. She has orchestrated a sensory performance, and it works.

The blessing itself (vv.27-29) is the most lavish speech in the chapter: dew, grain, wine, dominion over peoples, lordship over brothers, the curse-and-bless formula. It is covenant language; it echoes the Noahic blessing and anticipates the national promises. Isaac pours his whole *nefesh* into it. When he is done, it is done. It cannot come back.

---

### Verse 33 — "Isaac trembled very exceedingly" (*vayyecherad charadah gedolah*)

**וַיֶּחֱרַד יִצְחָק חֲרָדָה גְּדֹלָה עַד-מְאֹד** — "And Isaac trembled, a great trembling, exceedingly." This is one of the most vivid and precise uses of the cognate accusative in the Torah. **חֲרָדָה גְּדֹלָה עַד-מְאֹד** — the noun (*charadah*, trembling) from the same root as the verb (*vayyecherad*), then intensified by *gedolah* (great) and again by *'ad me'od* (exceedingly/to the extreme). The Leningrad Codex scripts this phrase in the clear Tiberian vocalization that the Masoretes used to mark significant emotional moments. The trembling is not merely shock. It is the earthquake of a man who realizes he has performed an irrevocable act on false premises — and that the act will stand anyway.

This moment is the pivot of the chapter. Everything before it is the setup; everything after it is consequence. Isaac does not undo the blessing; he cannot. "He will stay blessed" (*vayehi barukh*) — the passive perfect with a *vav*-consecutive makes this about as final as Hebrew prose can make it. The blessing has been released into the world. It has found its recipient. And Isaac's trembling is the moment he understands that what happened here was not entirely outside the purposes of God — even as his heart breaks for the son who is standing in front of him, empty-handed.

---

### Verse 36 — the Jacob/heel wordplay (*ya'aqov / 'aqav*)

**וַיֹּאמֶר הֲכִי קָרָא שְׁמוֹ יַעֲקֹב וַיַּעְקְבֵנִי זֶה פַעֲמַיִם** — "And he said: Is it not because his name is called Jacob? For he has supplanted me these two times." The verb **וַיַּעְקְבֵנִי** (*vayyaqeveni*) plays on the name **יַעֲקֹב** (*Ya'aqov*, Jacob) by invoking the root **עָקַב** (*'aqav*) — "to grab the heel, to supplant, to trip up." The name Jacob was given at birth because he came out clutching Esau's heel (Genesis 25:26). Now Esau uses the same root as a verb of bitter accusation: Jacob has acted like his name — the heel-grabber has grabbed *him* twice. Once with the birthright (for soup!), now with the blessing.

Esau's grievance is not merely personal injury. It is the accusation that Jacob has acted in character — that his name, which began as an observation (*he grabbed the heel*), has become a prophecy and a verdict (*he is a grabber, a supplanter, a cheat*). The wordplay is untranslatable without a note; I have preserved both the name and the meaning — "tripped me up" captures the *aqav* root's physical sense of hooking someone's heel to bring them down.

---

### Verses 39-40 — Esau's anti-blessing

**וַיַּעַן יִצְחָק אָבִיו וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו** — "And his father Isaac answered him..." The speech Isaac gives Esau in vv.39-40 is structurally parallel to Jacob's blessing in vv.27-29, but it is systematically inverted. Jacob got the dew of heaven and the richness of the earth; Esau is told his place will be *away from* the dew and *away from* the richness. The Hebrew **מִשְׁמַנֵּי הָאָרֶץ יִהְיֶה מוֹשָׁבֶךָ** at first glance could be read as "of the richness of the earth will be your dwelling" — but the preposition **מִ** here functions privatively, "away from the richness," as many scholars read it and as the context of the anti-blessing demands. Jacob was blessed with rule over brothers; Esau is given the sword and told he will serve. The one hope in Esau's speech is the prophecy of v.40b: **וְהָיָה כַּאֲשֶׁר תָּרִיד** — "and when you grow restless / break loose" — you will throw off the yoke. This is a prophecy of Edomite independence, which was historically fulfilled when Edom revolted against Israelite control (see 2 Kings 8:20-22).

---

### Verse 41 — the waiting murderer

**וַיִּשְׂטֹם עֵשָׂו אֶת-יַעֲקֹב עַל-הַבְּרָכָה אֲשֶׁר בֵּרֲכוֹ אָבִיו** — "And Esau hated Jacob because of the blessing with which his father had blessed him." The verb **שָׂטַם** (*satam*) is not the common verb for hate (**שָׂנֵא**, *sane*). It means to harbor a deep, sustained, nursing hatred — to bear a grudge that does not fade. The root appears only rarely in the Hebrew Bible but carries the weight of a wound that festers. It is a word for the kind of hatred that waits. Esau does not act immediately; he bides his time. He is waiting for his father to die. "The days of mourning for my father are near" — this is not grief-speak; it is a countdown.

The Vaticanus LXX renders this as **ἐνεκότει δὲ Ησαυ τῷ Ιακωβ** — "Esau bore a grudge against Jacob" — using *enekotei*, a verb for sustained inner resentment, which captures the same slow burn as the Hebrew *satam*.

---

### Verse 45 — "why should I be bereaved of both of you in one day?"

**לָמָה אֶשְׁכַּל גַּם-שְׁנֵיכֶם יוֹם אֶחָד** — "Why should I be bereaved of both of you in one day?" Rebekah's final words to Jacob here are the most nakedly maternal in the chapter: she is not worried about the birthright or the blessing or the covenant. She is worried about losing her sons. If Esau kills Jacob, the avenger of blood laws may result in Esau being killed in turn — or simply Esau will be beyond her reach. Either way, she will have lost both of them. She frames the separation as temporary: "stay for a while" (*yashavta... yamim achadim*), "a few days." She plans to send word when the time is right. The reader knows she never gets the chance. This is the last time Rebekah speaks in the book of Genesis. She sends Jacob away, and he does not return to her. The manipulation that kept him home has now sent him permanently away.

---

### Verse 46 — Rebekah's cover story

**וַתֹּאמֶר רִבְקָה אֶל-יִצְחָק קַצְתִּי בְחַיַּי מִפְּנֵי בְּנוֹת חֵת** — "And Rebekah said to Isaac: I loathe my life because of the daughters of Heth." This is Rebekah at her most calculating. She cannot tell Isaac the real reason she needs to send Jacob away — because Isaac does not know the full truth of what happened, and she cannot afford to tell him. So she pivots to the one grievance she and Isaac share: Esau's Hittite wives, who are already described in 26:35 as "bitterness of spirit" to both of them. She weaponizes that shared grief to get Isaac to send Jacob north, to Paddan-aram, to her brother Laban's household — where she has always intended Jacob to go. It is not a lie, exactly. She really does loathe her life because of the Hittite women. But the timing is entirely strategic. Rebekah is, to the end, the most intelligent person in every room she inhabits.

---

### The silence of God

One of the most striking features of Genesis 27 is that God does not appear, does not speak, and is not named except by Jacob — in a lie. When Jacob says "The LORD your God put it in my path" (v.20), this is the one invocation of the divine name in the chapter, and it is a false statement made to deceive. The LORD is absent from the drama as an active participant. He gave the oracle in Genesis 25:23. He does not intervene again here. He lets the family do what it will do — lets Isaac love the wrong son, lets Rebekah scheme, lets Jacob lie, lets Esau weep — and somehow, through all of this, the declared outcome arrives. "The older will serve the younger" comes true, but at a cost that will ripple through the family for twenty years.

The theology of this chapter is not comfortable. It does not present God as the tidy author of events but as the one who has declared an end and left human beings to thrash their way toward it through deception, grief, and broken relationships. The blessing lands where it was always going to land — but it lands in blood.

---

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 15r–15v, photographed by the West Semitic Research Project (Bruce E. Zuckerman). Genesis 27 occupies the majority of page 001 of this chapter (folio 15r, right and center columns), running from verse 1 through approximately verse 29, with the chapter concluding on page 002 (folio 15v) covering verses 30–46 before Genesis 28 begins. Secondary reference: Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana, pages 23–24 (accessible at https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0027 and /0028). The Vaticanus LXX follows the Hebrew closely throughout Genesis 27 with the following notable variations: v.22 — Vaticanus reads αἱ δὲ χεῖρες χεῖρες Ησαυ, close to the MT; v.33 — the LXX renders the trembling as ἐξέστη δὲ Ισαακ ἔκστασιν μεγάλην σφόδρα ("Isaac was seized with a great amazement/ecstasy exceedingly"), using ἔκστασις (ekstasis) rather than the word for physical trembling — a significant shift toward the metaphysical/numinous register rather than the physical; v.36 — the LXX reads ἐπτέρνικέν με ("he has tripped my heel"), preserving the wordplay in Greek with ptérna (heel); v.39 — the LXX reads ἀπὸ τῆς πιότητος τῆς γῆς ἔσται ἡ κατοίκησίς σου ("from the richness of the earth will be your habitation"), which is ambiguous between the privative and the constructive reading; v.41 — Vaticanus uses ἐνεκότει (bore a grudge), capturing the nuance of the Hebrew satam. No DSS fragments of Genesis 27 are known from the Qumran scrolls.*

---

*— PNEUMA*
